subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Holds a Master’s Degree completed at age 18, indicating accelerated academic progress.
Master's degree field is likely in finance, economics, or business, aligning with investment fund management.
Has undertaken coursework in investment analysis, portfolio management, and financial markets.
Participated in university projects or internships related to venture capital or startup evaluation.
No mention of professional certifications yet, consistent with early career stage.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Job title: Investment Fund Manager (entry-level).
Role focuses on sourcing high-quality startup projects for potential investment.
Frequently communicates with entrepreneurs to explain investment criteria and processes.
Responsible for preparing industry research reports and staying informed on technology trends.
Coordinates with internal and external stakeholders via digital platforms.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Works in the finance sector, with a specialization in venture capital and investment management.
Focuses on sourcing and evaluating startups, primarily within the technology sector.
Industry role bridges finance and technology, requiring awareness of both markets.
Engages with entrepreneurs and startup founders, often in early-stage tech ventures.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Works at a small-to-mid-sized investment fund, typical for early-career fund managers.
Company environment is fast-paced and collaborative, reflecting the startup-focused investment space.
Team structure likely involves close interaction with a handful of senior managers and analysts.
Remote and digital communication are integral, utilizing platforms like Feishu, WeChat, and LinkedIn.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Business operates as a venture capital or private equity fund.
Focuses on early-stage and growth-stage startup investments, primarily in technology.
Market segment emphasizes high-growth, innovative companies.
Regularly assesses market trends and emerging technologies for investment opportunities.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has less than 1 year of experience in the current investment fund manager role.
Started the role immediately after completing a Master’s degree.
Rapid transition from education to professional investment work.
Brings fresh academic insights but is still developing practical industry expertise.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Holds an entry-level professional position within the investment fund.
Acts under guidance from more senior fund managers and partners.
Role provides significant learning and exposure to industry best practices.
Expected to rapidly build expertise and take on greater responsibility over time.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Sources and screens startup projects for potential investment (majority of daily workload).
Communicates investment processes and criteria to entrepreneurs via digital channels.
Conducts in-depth industry research, particularly in the technology sector.
Prepares and writes research reports to inform fund decision-makers.
Coordinates with team members and maintains communication records using Feishu.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Devotes approximately 75% of time to sourcing projects and explaining investment matters.
Spends about 25% of time on industry research and report writing.
Regularly balances inbound startup pitches with proactive market scouting.
Splits communication tasks between entrepreneurs (external) and colleagues (internal).

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Operates with moderate autonomy in initial project sourcing and screening.
Final investment decisions are made by senior managers or investment committee.
Independently manages day-to-day communication with entrepreneurs.
Relies on guidance for complex evaluations or high-value investment opportunities.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Quickly identifies promising startup projects using established screening criteria.
Efficient at digital communication with entrepreneurs via WeChat and Feishu.
Comfortable navigating LinkedIn to expand professional network.
Finds it straightforward to summarize basic industry news for internal reports.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Evaluating startups with complex or unfamiliar business models requires extra effort.
Writing detailed, in-depth industry research reports can be time-consuming.
Assessing founder credibility and potential for success is nuanced and difficult.
Synthesizing large volumes of market data into actionable insights poses challenges.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Initial data gathering on startups (e.g., scraping basic information) is highly repetitive.
Compiling lists and basic profiles of market competitors can be automated.
Routine formatting and updating of industry reports is data-heavy and suitable for AI tools.
Filtering inbound startup inquiries for relevance could be delegated to automation.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Most enjoys direct conversations with passionate entrepreneurs.
Finds researching new technology trends intellectually stimulating.
Feels bored by manual data entry and report formatting.
Tedious tasks include chasing down missing information from applicants.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Manual collation of startup data from multiple sources is time-consuming.
Information silos across different communication platforms can cause delays.
Occasional duplication of work due to lack of integration between Feishu and LinkedIn.
Difficulty in quickly validating self-reported data from entrepreneurs.

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Productivity is highest when working on tasks that are both enjoyable and well-practiced, such as networking online.
Skill level in digital research and communication directly increases task enjoyment.
Repetitive, low-skill tasks are less enjoyable and tend to be completed less efficiently.
Challenging tasks are more enjoyable when they align with personal learning goals.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Enthusiastic about adopting the latest digital tools, especially AI/LLM platforms.
Views technology learning as essential for career advancement in finance.
Regularly experiments with new productivity apps and research tools.
Actively seeks out best practices for using communication platforms.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Adapts quickly to new software, given regular exposure throughout education and early career.
Comfortable with frequent updates or changes to existing work platforms.
Able to troubleshoot basic issues with digital tools independently.
Shows resilience in overcoming initial learning curves.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Willing to dedicate time outside of work hours for self-guided training on AI and research tools.
Participates in webinars and online tutorials relevant to investment and tech.
Prefers concise, actionable training materials over lengthy manuals.
Open to peer-led training sessions for new internal tools.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Motivated by the potential for increased efficiency and accuracy in work.
Seeks to maintain a competitive edge by staying updated on technology.
Heavy workload can occasionally delay deep dives into unfamiliar tools.
Relevance to daily tasks is a key factor in prioritizing which technologies to learn.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Peers who champion new tech inspire quicker adoption.
Positive management attitudes toward innovation encourage experimentation.
Team discussions about tool effectiveness influence individual adoption decisions.
Management provides recommendations for AI tools to streamline workflow.

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses Feishu (Lark) as the primary platform for internal communication and document collaboration.
WeChat is used extensively for external communication with entrepreneurs.
Leverages LinkedIn for professional networking and sourcing potential startup founders.
Utilizes AI-driven language models (LLMs) for research and report drafting.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Relies on Feishu for task management and process tracking.
Uses WeChat for real-time messaging and voice calls with startup founders.
Employs LinkedIn to keep abreast of industry trends and make new connections.
May use spreadsheet software (e.g., Excel) for basic data analysis.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Familiar with CRM systems commonly used in investment management, though not the primary tool.
Understands the basics of data rooms and secure document sharing platforms.
Has been exposed to financial modeling tools during academic training.
Comfortable with widely-used communication and collaboration platforms.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Lack of seamless integration between Feishu, WeChat, and LinkedIn creates workflow friction.
Must manually transfer data between platforms for comprehensive project tracking.
Occasional challenges synchronizing contact information or conversation histories.
AI tools may not directly integrate with all communication platforms, requiring copy-paste workflows.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Understands basic concepts such as natural language processing, machine learning, and prompt engineering.
Regularly uses terminology like 'LLM,' 'fine-tuning,' and 'knowledge graph' when discussing AI tools.
Able to explain how LLMs are trained and their limitations in generating factual content.
Has explored prompt optimization for more accurate research outputs.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Uses ChatGPT or similar LLM platforms for drafting research reports.
Employs Feishu's built-in AI features for meeting summaries and document editing.
Occasionally tests new AI-driven tools suggested by colleagues, such as Notion AI or Perplexity.
May use language translation AI for cross-border communications.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Applies AI tools daily for research, summarization, and report drafting.
Uses LLMs to generate executive summaries and investment memos.
Relies on AI for quickly extracting market trends from large datasets.
Employs AI-driven chatbots to automate initial screening of startup applications.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: Successfully used LLMs to produce a comprehensive industry research report in record time.
Noted that AI-generated summaries occasionally missed nuanced insights, requiring manual review.
Experienced minor factual inaccuracies in AI outputs, leading to stricter verification processes.
Learned to iterate on prompts for more relevant and accurate AI responses.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: Relies primarily on self-guided learning and online resources to develop AI skills.
Peers occasionally share tips and effective prompts in team chats.
No formal internal AI training program yet, consistent with small company environment.
Management supports professional development but has not mandated AI certification.

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers step-by-step collaboration with AI tools when tackling complex research tasks.
Uses LLMs independently for quick queries and routine report drafts.
Iterative feedback loop between human and AI is favored for refining outputs.
Adjusts interaction style based on task complexity and urgency.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Views AI primarily as an assistant for data processing and report writing.
Treats LLMs as a research advisor when exploring new market segments.
Occasionally uses AI as a co-worker to brainstorm investment criteria.
Sees human judgment as the final authority on important decisions.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Highly open to experimenting with new AI workflows to improve efficiency.
Actively seeks ways to delegate repetitive tasks to automation.
Open-minded about integrating AI into communication platforms.
Prefers to pilot new workflows before full adoption.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Prefers conversational interfaces for brainstorming and exploratory research.
Uses command-based prompts for structured tasks, such as report formatting.
Finds natural language queries more intuitive for complex information retrieval.
Enjoys blended interfaces that allow both conversational and direct commands.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Startup evaluation often depends on nuanced market understanding not easily replicated by AI.
Assessing founder credibility and team dynamics requires tacit knowledge.
Some financial modeling tasks are standardized; others are highly context-dependent.
Relies on human judgment for final investment recommendations.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Trusts AI for initial drafts and data aggregation, but always reviews outputs.
AI-generated insights are considered a starting point rather than final authority.
Critical investment decisions are never made solely based on AI recommendations.
Trust increases with transparent AI processes and clear source attribution.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers high automation for data collection and basic report writing.
Favors human-led analysis and decision-making for investment evaluations.
Sees optimal workflow as human-AI collaboration, blending speed and expertise.
Wants to automate repetitive tasks to free up time for high-value work.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: Accepts high automation for non-critical, repetitive, or data-heavy tasks.
Would find automation threatening if it compromised decision transparency.
Maintains manual checks for tasks with financial or reputational risk.
Supports automation that enhances, not replaces, human expertise.

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: Believes AI enhances the value of analytical and critical thinking skills.
Routine research and data entry skills are becoming less critical due to automation.
Communication and relationship-building remain essential and irreplaceable.
Sees a shift toward skills in prompt engineering and AI oversight.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Prompt engineering and effective AI tool usage are new core competencies.
Responsible for validating and contextualizing AI-generated content.
Needs to understand data privacy and ethical considerations in AI deployment.
Developing skills in integrating AI with traditional research methodologies.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Low concern about AI replacing core investment decision-making tasks.
Expects some repetitive research and reporting tasks to be fully automated.
Believes human judgment will remain central to investment management.
Views AI as a tool for augmentation rather than replacement.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: Always reviews and edits AI-generated reports before distribution.
Manual verification of key data points is standard practice.
Ensures human sign-off on all investment recommendations.
Can revert to manual workflows if AI outputs are unreliable.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: Team is increasingly open to piloting new AI tools.
No formal AI adoption policy yet, but discussions are ongoing.
Peers are encouraged to share AI best practices.
Company culture supports experimentation but emphasizes human oversight.

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Believes AI will dramatically increase efficiency in finance and investment sectors.
Recognizes potential for AI to democratize access to financial insights.
Cautiously optimistic about AI's ability to drive innovation.
Aware of risks related to job displacement and ethical misuse.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Supports transparent AI processes and clear accountability.
Concerned about potential biases in AI-generated recommendations.
Values data privacy and secure handling of sensitive information.
Advocates for human review in high-stakes decision making.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Desires seamless integration of LLMs with Feishu, WeChat, and LinkedIn for unified workflows.
Wants AI tools capable of real-time validation of startup claims.
Seeks advanced AI summarization for multi-language industry reports.
Would benefit from AI-driven market trend prediction tailored to venture investments.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Expects routine screening and reporting tasks to be highly automated.
Anticipates greater emphasis on creative analysis and strategic relationship-building.
Foresees need for continual upskilling in AI tool usage and oversight.
Predicts tighter collaboration between human analysts and AI systems.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Develop clear guidelines for responsible AI adoption and oversight.
Invest in staff training on advanced AI functionalities.
Encourage cross-team sharing of AI use cases and best practices.
Pilot integrated AI tools for report generation and communication management.
